title: Offices of Real Estate Agents and Brokers

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 531210 ($30,836,359,913) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 531210 ($180,013,344,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Property, Real Estate, and Community Association Managers
Janitors and Cleaners, Except Maids and Housekeeping Cleaners
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.0396 (margins.json[53]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Real Estate (Operations & Services) EBITDA/Sales 3.96% (agency/brokerage/property-management economics, dominant in firm counts)); revenue/employee $432,174 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 531210); class <5: 140,557 firms, avg revenue/firm $449,562 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,803 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,638 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,862,814 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $113,367 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,095 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,465,620 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $256,039 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,204 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,484,213 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $652,775 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 259 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,286,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,912,143 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 139 firms, avg revenue/firm $373,541,367 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,792,238 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 259
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Real Estate (Operations & Services) EBITDA/Sales 3.96% (agency/brokerage/property-management economics, dominant in firm counts)

breakdown_by_role.role: Maintenance and Repair Workers, General (49-9071) — CONTAMINATION
breakdown_by_role.rationale: ROLE_MIX CONTAMINATION: This role is present in 3-digit 531 due to sibling codes 531110/531120 (lessors) and 531311/531312 (property managers) which employ maintenance staff. Maintenance and repair workers do not exist in 531210 brokerage offices. Automatable fraction set to 0.0 per prompt instruction to not build the V breakdown on contaminating roles.

breakdown_by_role.role: Property, Real Estate, and Community Association Managers (11-9141) — CONTAMINATION
breakdown_by_role.rationale: ROLE_MIX CONTAMINATION: Property/community association managers are from 531311/531312 property management firms, not 531210 brokerage offices. The largest single occupation in the 3-digit 531 proxy by wage share, but largely absent from pure brokerage operations. Automatable fraction set to 0.0 per prompt instruction.

W-2 salaried agents only (the vast majority of agents are 1099 independent contractors outside the QCEW wage bill entirely). Tasks that automate: listing preparation (CMA/pricing, marketing copy, listing photos descriptions), disclosure document drafting, scheduling, database management. Tasks that persist: holding state license to collect commission (legal requirement), negotiation, client trust in high-stakes transaction, showing properties. 40% reflects the document/data preparation portion vs. the licensed-relationship-negotiation core.

ROLE_MIX CONTAMINATION: Counter and rental clerks are present in 531 due to rental/leasing sibling codes. In a 531210 brokerage office, this occupation is essentially absent. Automatable fraction set to 0.0.

Transaction coordination, listing administration, document filing, scheduling. Highly automatable with current AI and transaction management software (Dotloop, SkySlope, DocuSign). 75% leaves 25% for escalations and complex coordination.

Managing-broker role is statutory: a licensed broker must supervise agents and sign off on transactions — this core cannot be automated. Operational reporting, recruiting analytics, office scheduling, and financial reporting automate. 25% reflects partial automation of the non-licensed management activities.
ROLE_MIX CONTAMINATION: Physical cleaning/maintenance from sibling codes. Not present in brokerage offices. Automatable fraction set to 0.0.
Scheduling, correspondence, document preparation, calendar management. Highly automatable. 70% leaves 30% for complex client communications and stakeholder coordination.

Commission disbursement accounting, trust accounting, agent splits, expense tracking. Highly automatable with AI accounting tools and brokerage management software. 80% leaves 20% for complex reconciliations and exception handling.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers (49-1011) — CONTAMINATION
breakdown_by_role.rationale: ROLE_MIX CONTAMINATION: Supervisors of maintenance/repair workers from property management sibling codes. Not present in 531210 brokerage offices. Automatable fraction set to 0.0.

breakdown_by_role.role: Remaining unlisted roles (marketing, IT, HR, transaction coordinators, leasing agents in tail)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The remaining 37.82% of wage bill consists of marketing staff, IT support, HR, and additional brokerage support roles. Conservative 40% (vs. higher estimate) applied because: (1) this tail of the 3-digit 531 proxy likely still includes some contaminating property-management roles; (2) brokerage marketing is partially automatable (listing marketing, social media, lead generation) but includes brand strategy and agent recruitment; (3) IT and HR have lower automation fractions. 40% reflects a blended estimate acknowledging continued contamination risk in the unlisted tail.

pi_dist.rationale: Client relationship control is extremely weak in brokerage: (1) agent books are personal and fully portable — when a brokerage is acquired, agents can re-affiliate the next day and take their clients with them; (2) upstream gatekeeping by Zillow/Realtor.com/MLS controls consumer search and increasingly monetizes leads before they reach any brokerage; (3) the 2024 NAR settlement decoupled buyer-agent compensation, increasing buyer awareness of commission costs and enabling direct negotiation; (4) the brokerage retains only 'company dollar' (~10-30% of GCI), while 70-90% passes through to agents — meaning the brokerage's share of the commission is already thin; (5) flat-fee MLS platforms (Houzeo, List With Freedom) and AI-enhanced direct listing services provide increasing substitutes. pi_dist = 0.35 reflecting that the brokerage-level entity has very limited client lock-in and faces structural disintermediation risk.
sources: NAR settlement implementation documentation (August 2024) — buyer-agent commission decoupled from MLS
ctacquisitions.com Real Estate Brokerage Business Valuation 2026 — agent portability as primary risk factor
HousingWire NAR commission settlement analysis 2025 — post-settlement commission rate data

pi_moat.rationale: Barrier strength is moderate but declining: (1) State licensing requires a licensed broker to supervise agents and collect commissions — a real legal barrier but not protection against AI-native licensed platforms; (2) consumer trust in a high-stakes transaction provides some stickiness for full-service agents, but this is not a firm-level moat (it attaches to the individual agent); (3) MLSs and portals control the data infrastructure that the brokerage depends on — no proprietary data advantage; (4) brand is mostly franchise-driven (Coldwell Banker, Century 21, RE/MAX), not organic brokerage IP; (5) switching costs are low for both agents (re-affiliation is routine) and consumers (who transact rarely and shop on portals). Threat from AI-native transaction services is growing. pi_moat = 0.40 reflecting modest but structurally weakening barriers.
sources: NAR 2025 Technology Survey — 68% agent AI adoption documenting tech infrastructure shift
inman.com consolidation analysis 2026 — documents Compass/Anywhere and eXp/NextHome mergers as defensive moves
ctacquisitions.com Real Estate Brokerage Valuation 2026 — notes NAR settlement compressed multiples 0.5-1x EBITDA

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption baseline: 20% of agents use AI daily (NAR 2025 Technology Survey; daily use is the 'material' threshold). Historical analog: eSignature in real estate went from ~20% adoption (~2014) to 79% (2025) in approximately 11 years; but it started slower with a COVID acceleration spike. CRM went from ~30% to 72% in ~8 years. AI is adopting faster than prior real estate tech: 68% already use it at some level (any frequency) and 66%+ plan to increase use. From 20% daily/material to 50% material adoption: at the current trajectory of strong intent and falling tool costs, approximately 4-6 years. t50 = 5.0 years (mid-range). Note: 'material adoption' for this code primarily means embedding AI in listing preparation, CMA generation, and transaction document drafting — the automatable W-2 tasks. The 1099 agent majority adopting AI is relevant context but does not flow through the labor_share basis.

current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 68% of agents have used AI tools (20% daily, 22% weekly, 27% a few times monthly, 32% not yet using). Daily use (20%) taken as material adoption threshold; any-use 68% vastly overstates embedded workflow adoption.

historical_analogs.value: 8-11 years to 50% for eSignature and CRM in real estate; AI is adopting approximately 2x faster due to lower tool costs and COVID-era digital acceleration setting the baseline
historical_analogs.source: NAR REALTOR Technology Survey historical series (2022 and 2025 editions)
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: eSignature now at 79% adoption (2025); CRM at 72.5% adoption (2025); these tools were at ~50% roughly 6-8 years earlier based on NAR survey series progression

owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 44% of REALTORS are 60 or older (up from 35% the prior year); median age is 57; only 11% of agents are under 40

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: NAR's 'silver tsunami' coverage documents that 44% of Realtors are 60+ (up from 35% in prior year) with median age 57. Industry commentary documents that when broker-owners retire, agents re-affiliate rather than buying the brokerage, creating structural dissolution rather than succession. The 259 n_band firms (100-499 employee tier) face this risk but it manifests as business dissolution rather than classic succession shortage.

active_consolidators.source: inman.com consolidation analysis (May 2026) and HousingWire coverage of Compass/Anywhere and eXp/NextHome mergers
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Primary institutional consolidators: Compass (acquired Anywhere 2026), eXp Realty (acquired NextHome), Real Brokerage (pursued REMAX), Side (back-office platform), Rocket/Redfin, plus ~3-5 regional PE-backed brokerage platforms actively acquiring. Far fewer than insurance: PE finds brokerage economics unattractive due to agent portability — agents leave with their books post-acquisition.

buy_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com Real Estate Brokerage Business Valuation 2026 (citing post-settlement transaction data)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Multi-office regional independent ($1M-$10M EBITDA): 2.5-4x EBITDA post-settlement; buyers underwriting 5-15% commission compression over 24-36 months compressed multiples 0.5-1x from pre-settlement levels; mid-point 3.0x used. EBITDA normalization: based on company dollar retained after agent splits, not gross commission income.

exit_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com Real Estate Brokerage Business Valuation 2026 and HousingWire Compass/Anywhere deal coverage
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Multi-office institutional platform ($10M+ EBITDA): 4-6x EBITDA; Compass/Anywhere transaction implies platform-scale pricing at high end; mid-point 5.0x used. Strategic buyers (Compass, eXp) may pay occasionally higher for tuck-in acquisitions but these are not arm's-length EBITDA transactions.

pricing_structure.value: Commission-based: ~5-6% of transaction value (listing agent 2.7-3%, buyer agent 2.4-2.7% as of 2025 post-settlement). 70-90% of gross commission income passes to 1099 agents under commission splits. Brokerage retains 'company dollar' of 10-30% of GCI, translating to ~3.96% EBITDA on gross receipts per Damodaran. Post-2024 settlement, buyer-agent compensation must be negotiated separately and disclosed in buyer representation agreements. AI efficiency savings on the thin W-2 cost base flow to brokerage margin but are tiny relative to the commission pool.
pricing_structure.source: Redfin commission rate analysis (HousingWire, 2025) and NAR settlement documentation
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Average buyer's agent commission was 2.40% in Q1 2025 vs 2.37% in late 2024 — minimal compression post-settlement; listing agent ~2.77%; typical total commission ~5.44% per Clever survey of 806 agents

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = V_raw × 100 = 0.048572 × 100 = 4.86pp. A=2.0 (t50=5 years, moderately fast). 4.86pp uplift is above the 3pp tension threshold with A=2.0 (threshold applies when A>=5, which is not the case here). No tension: the small uplift reflects the structural constraint that labor_share is only 0.1826 — the W-2 cost base is thin because most agents are 1099 outside the wage bill. Even fully automating all W-2 labor would free only ~18pp of revenue, not 25pp. The low V (1.94) is an accurate arithmetic result of this structural limitation, not a calibration error.

terminal_value.justification: Two separate mechanisms must be distinguished. (1) AI cutting brokerage W-2 costs: the automatable base is small (labor_share 0.1826, and contamination means true brokerage W-2 costs are even smaller). This produces a real but modest margin improvement. (2) AI replacing the brokerage's product (licensed intermediation of a home sale): this is the more dangerous mechanism. AI already automates listing preparation, CMA/pricing, marketing copy, disclosure documents, and scheduling — historically ~70% of what an agent's day involves. The statutory holdout is that a licensed agent must collect a commission and must sign off on transactions. The practical holdout is consumer trust in the largest financial transaction of their lives. Post-2024 NAR settlement, buyer-agent compensation is decoupled — buyers must explicitly negotiate and pay their agent, increasing their awareness of the cost. Compass/Anywhere's $10B merger and eXp/NextHome consolidation suggest the industry is reshaping around scale and tech platforms, not protecting the traditional brokerage model. Commission rates have NOT materially compressed post-settlement (Q1 2025: 2.40% buyer agent vs. 2.37% pre-settlement per HousingWire/Clever), suggesting the holdout is holding — for now. Overall: PRESSURED. The traditional full-commission brokerage model faces structural headwinds from AI-enhanced consumer self-service and settlement-enabled direct negotiation, but the licensed-intermediation requirement and consumer trust stickiness prevent rapid collapse. Commission pool may compress 10-20% over the decade, primarily in buyer-agent fees, while listing side remains more stable.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was set from the 3-digit 531 OEWS occupational wage shares with explicit zero-fraction assignments to contaminating occupations (maintenance workers, property managers, counter clerks, janitors, mechanic supervisors) that are not present in 531210 brokerage offices. The within_role_automatable fractions for genuine brokerage occupations were set based on task analysis of brokerage workflows, not on M&A deal activity. A was derived from the NAR 2025 Technology Survey (20% daily AI adoption baseline) and historical analogs from NAR technology survey series data, with no reference to consolidation trends or deal volumes.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Brokerage revenue is ~5-6% commission on transaction value. 70-90% of gross commission income passes directly to 1099 independent contractor agents under commission splits — the brokerage retains only 'company dollar' (10-30% of GCI), which is the actual revenue base for EBITDA after splits. This creates a fundamental structural issue: AI savings on the thin W-2 labor base (office clerks, bookkeepers, admin) flow to the brokerage's margin on the company dollar, but represent a tiny fraction of the total commission pool. The commission rate itself is set by market competition (buyer and seller negotiation), not by brokerage cost structure — so AI efficiency savings on W-2 costs do NOT flow to consumers as lower commission rates (the commission rate is a market-clearing price, not a cost-plus). However, if AI enables agent productivity improvements that allow agents to serve more clients at the same quality, competitive pressure may eventually compress commission rates. This systemic risk is reflected in pi_dist=0.35 (thin company dollar, agent portability) and pi_moat=0.40 (market pricing risk, portal/MLS gatekeeping of lead flow). Post-settlement, buyer-agent fees are explicitly negotiated — a mechanism that could accelerate fee compression if buyers use AI tools to evaluate whether the fee is justified.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=4: A=2.5, S rises to ~2.62. At t50=8: A=1.25, S falls to ~2.35. Central t50=5 gives A=2.0, S=2.51. Modest swing of ~0.27 points in S given the low V and C scores constrain total.

At buy_mult=2.0 with exit 5.0: M=clamp(4×3/2,0,10)=clamp(6.0,0,10)=6.0, S rises to ~2.88. At buy_mult=4.5: M=clamp(4×0.5/4.5,0,10)=0.444, S falls to ~1.66. Largest swing of any input given M sensitivity.

At 0.15: V_raw=0.1826×0.15=0.02739, V=10×0.02739/0.25=1.096, S falls to ~2.21. At 0.35: V_raw=0.1826×0.35=0.06391, V=10×0.06391/0.25=2.556, S rises to ~2.76. Key uncertainty is how much of the remaining 37.82% unlisted tail is contamination vs. genuine brokerage roles.
At pi_dist=0.25: C=10×0.25×0.40=1.0, S falls to ~2.36. At pi_dist=0.50: C=10×0.50×0.40=2.0, S rises to ~2.68. Agent portability and portal gatekeeping are the key judgment inputs here.

reviewer_note: ROLE_MIX CONTAMINATION (CRITICAL): The 3-digit 531 proxy role_mix is dominated by occupations from sibling codes — maintenance workers (12.89% of wage bill), property/community association managers (15.49%), counter/rental clerks (3.70%), janitors (2.40%), and mechanic supervisors (2.82%) — totaling ~37.3% of the proxy wage bill — that are essentially absent from 531210 brokerage offices. Per prompt instruction, these contaminating roles are assigned within_role_automatable_fraction = 0.0. The genuine brokerage occupations (W-2 sales agents, office managers, clerks, bookkeepers, admin assistants) total only ~23.5% of the proxy wage bill. The remaining unlisted 37.82% tail also likely contains contamination from the 531 composite. This creates significant downward pressure on ai_replaceable_share and hence V, which is arithmetically correct given the structural reality: the true brokerage W-2 base is small because most agents are 1099. STRUCTURAL MISMATCH: The roll-up screen formula assumes the automation value accrues to the W-2 labor base. For 531210, this misses the dominant cost driver: the commission pass-through to 1099 agents (which cannot be automated away — it's contractual). AI tools adopted by agents improve individual agent productivity but that value accrues to the agent (via more transactions), not the brokerage (which keeps the same company-dollar percentage). This is the most fundamental reason this code scores low. EBITDA NORMALIZATION: The 3.96% Damodaran margin applies to gross receipts. On the brokerage's retained company dollar (10-30% of GCI), EBITDA margins are much higher (~15-25%), and the n_band=259 firms are the 100-499 employee multi-office platforms that have retained stronger company-dollar economics than small single-agent shops. The buy/exit multiples (3.0x/5.0x) are calibrated to this company-dollar EBITDA basis. NAR SETTLEMENT: Post-2024 settlement (effective August 2024), buyer-agent compensation was decoupled from MLS listings. Commission data through Q1 2025 shows minimal compression (2.40% buyer agent vs. 2.37% pre-settlement), but structural pressure remains. Buyers who explicitly negotiate buyer-agent fees may increasingly use AI to evaluate cost vs. value, creating long-term compression risk. This is reflected in the PRESSURED terminal-value classification.
